question: Why can't a fast origin server alone give a distant user a fast experience?
options: Because servers get tired
Because round-trip latency is bounded by the speed of light over distance — bytes spend most of the time in flight across the ocean
Because DNS is slow
Because the user's browser is slow
explanation: A Sydney-to-Virginia round trip is ~160 ms of pure propagation at best. No matter how fast the origin answers, the distance dominates. The fix is to stop sending requests across the planet — cache near the user.

question: What is a CDN, in one sentence?
options: A faster type of database
A globally distributed fleet of edge caching servers in PoPs near users, so requests hit a nearby edge instead of your distant origin
A load balancer inside your datacenter
A DNS provider
explanation: A Content Delivery Network places caching servers (edges) in Points of Presence worldwide. Users are routed to the nearest edge, which serves cached content and only falls back to origin on a miss.

question: How does anycast route a user to the nearest edge?
options: It asks the user to pick a region
The same IP is announced from every PoP, and internet routing (BGP) delivers the packet to the topologically nearest announcement
It uses a different IP per user
It sends the request to all edges at once
explanation: With anycast, hundreds of PoPs advertise one identical IP. BGP naturally routes each user's packets to the closest one, so 'nearest server' is decided by the network with no per-user logic.

question: Why does a CDN use an origin shield (a middle tier) between edges and origin?
options: To store more data
So a hot object expiring doesn't trigger hundreds of simultaneous edge-to-origin fetches — the shield collapses many edge misses into one origin fetch (global single-flight)
To terminate TLS
To pick the nearest edge
explanation: Without a shield, 300 edges each missing would stampede the origin. The shield is a regional intermediary all edges consult on a miss, collapsing the herd into one origin fetch — lesson 6's idea at planetary scale.

question: Why is fingerprinting a static asset's filename (app.9f8c2a.js) with a near-infinite TTL a best practice?
options: It makes files smaller
The content hash is in the URL, so a change produces a NEW URL — caches keep the old one forever safely and fetch the new one automatically, with no purge and no stale risk
It encrypts the file
It forces revalidation on every request
explanation: Versioned URLs are immutable: change the content, change the URL. You can cache each version forever; the browser/edge fetches the new filename after a deploy and the old one simply ages out — no purge call, no staleness.

question: What's the dangerous pitfall of marking a personalized page Cache-Control: public on a CDN?
options: It uses more bandwidth
A shared edge cache stores one user's authenticated response and serves it to a different user requesting the same URL — a data breach
The page loads slower
Nothing; public is always safe
explanation: Shared caches are shared. Marking authenticated/personalized content public lets the edge hand user A's data to user B. Such responses must be private/no-store (or keyed so they can never be shared).
